digest.intro: Code on clothes, dragonfly dogfights, and agents leaking your repos.

items.title: U.S. Strikes Iran as Hormuz Shipping Attacks Escalate
items.blurb: The most kinetic moment of the Iran conflict in weeks: U.S. military strikes landed hours after three merchant ships were hit in the Strait of Hormuz, the chokepoint for roughly 20% of global oil. This is happening simultaneously with the NATO summit in Ankara — two pressure points on the same day, each feeding the other's urgency.

items.title: NATO in Ankara: What the Alliance Actually Means When Its Anchor Power Is Transactional
items.blurb: Trump is at the Ankara summit enforcing spending pledges while Ukraine's future security arrangements hang in the balance — the year's hinge moment for European order. The question being answered in real time is whether NATO is a mutual-defense pact or a protection racket with a sliding fee.

items.title: Le Pen Convicted, Electronic Monitor On, Declares 2027 Run Anyway
items.blurb: France's leading democratic institutions have fitted their most popular presidential candidate with an ankle monitor — and she's running anyway. This is a live stress test of whether courts can actually disqualify a candidate without making her a martyr, with every European populist movement watching closely.

sections.heading: The agentic attack surface opens up

items.title: HalluSquatting: Attackers Pre-Register the Domains AI Will Hallucinate
items.blurb: Palo Alto's Unit 42 mapped 250,000 phantom domains — URLs that popular LLMs confidently hallucinate as real package sources or dependencies — and found attackers are registering them in advance to intercept whatever the AI fetches. The structural insight is the disturbing part: LLM failure modes are now *predictable enough to be pre-exploited* at scale, enabling the first pull-based prompt injection botnets.

items.title: GitLost: A Public GitHub Issue That Exfiltrates Your Private Repos
items.blurb: Noma Labs found that GitHub's new Agentic Workflows — where an AI agent reads issues and calls tools autonomously — could be hijacked by posting a crafted issue in *any public repo belonging to the same org* as your private ones. The agent reads the malicious instructions, trusts them, and silently ships private data out; no authentication required from the attacker.

items.title: Dragonfly Dogfights Run on Surprisingly Simple Vision Rules
items.blurb: Researchers set up a stereo-camera rig at a pond and reconstructed 102 paired male-on-male dragonfly flight trajectories, finding the aerial dogfighting behavior emerges from one simple goal: maintain a tactical position relative to the opponent, mirroring human fighter-pilot doctrine. The implication for drone guidance is that you don't need complex computation to produce stunning, adaptive maneuvers — just the right minimal rule applied fast.

items.title: The Anti-Trampoline Effect
items.blurb: Steve Mould reliably finds the physical phenomenon that breaks your naive model and then explains exactly why — here, a bouncy ball on a bouncy surface produces the opposite of what you'd expect. Worth 10 minutes for the kinetic intuition it rewires.

items.title: The Akamai Anti-Bot Script Printed on a Uniqlo T-Shirt
items.blurb: A Uniqlo t-shirt sold in high-street stores has a shebang line and a base64-encoded, self-evaluating bash script printed on the back — an Akamai easter egg hidden in a mass-market garment, structured exactly like malware delivery. The author had to do careful OCR to decode it because base64 has no error correction, and one wrong character breaks the whole thing. As an object it's genuinely strange: corporate infrastructure obfuscation made wearable and sold to civilians.

items.title: Amauta Magazine Covers, 1926–30: A Visual Language for Indigenous Modernism
items.blurb: José Carlos Mariátegui's Lima magazine — 100 years old this year — ran 32 issues and published Freud translations, César Vallejo poetry, Diego Rivera art, and Rosa Luxemburg reportage while inventing a visual identity that fused Andean indigenous iconography with European avant-garde design. The covers are the artifact: a case study in how an aesthetic system can carry political identity and create a movement that outlasts the magazine itself.

facts.fact: Palo Alto Networks' Unit 42 mapped 250,000 phantom domains that AI tools hallucinate as real — attackers are pre-registering them to intercept whatever the model fetches.

Male dragonflies engage in aerial dogfights using the same tactical positioning doctrine as human fighter pilots — and researchers reconstructed 102 such flight trajectories from a portable stereo-camera rig at a pond.

A Uniqlo t-shirt designed by Akamai contains a working, obfuscated bash script on the back — structured identically to how malware is typically delivered — that decodes to a benign Easter egg message.

At Üçağızlı II Cave in Turkey, Neanderthals and modern humans occupied the same site across different periods separated by millennia, yet left behind nearly identical stone tools and hunted the same animals — the species transition is only detectable by examining teeth.

GitHub's Agentic Workflows can be hijacked by posting a malicious issue in a *public* repository — no authentication needed — causing the AI agent to silently exfiltrate data from private repositories in the same organization.

art_picks.title: The Host and the Cloud
art_picks.creator: Pierre Huyghe
art_picks.why: A 2009–2010 film shot inside a deserted Paris ethnography museum on three holiday nights, where Huyghe staged ritualized scenes with paid participants — art as anthropology experiment, feedback loop as medium. It's the clearest window into his obsession with systems that blur fiction, institution, and human behavior before you see UUmwelt upstairs at MoMA.

art_picks.title: Lothar Baumgarten: Unfinished Map
art_picks.creator: Lothar Baumgarten
art_picks.why: Baumgarten spent years embedded with Yanomami communities in Venezuela, then made work from that encounter — language as landscape, cartography as violence, the museum as colonial instrument made visible. Obsessive, narrow, unreasonably deep, and formally beautiful. His permanent installation at the Guggenheim (the 'America Invention' floor text) turns the rotunda into a monument to erasure.

art_picks.title: Anima
art_picks.creator: Thom Yorke
art_picks.why: Released with a Paul Thomas Anderson one-reeler meant to be watched as a single unit — the record is a system, not a playlist: score-like electronic textures that render anxiety and urban drift as physical sensation. The physical/digital edge lives right here.

events.name: Pierre Huyghe: UUmwelt
events.venue: Museum of Modern Art (Abby Aldrich Rockefeller Sculpture Garden), Midtown Manhattan
events.why: Huyghe collaborated with Kyoto neuroscientists to feed fMRI brain-scan data into a neural network that then generates hallucinatory visual interpretations of human thought — displayed on freestanding screens in MoMA's sculpture garden, it dissolves the boundary between human and machine cognition in one of the most consequential shows in New York this year.

events.name: Gabriel Orozco: Public Nature
events.venue: JCDecaux Bus Shelters across New York City (citywide)
events.why: Orozco's first-ever commissioned photographic series — 12 images across 300 bus shelters, each a trompe l'oeil designed so the photograph bleeds into its surroundings: a tree growing through a concrete wall appears to extend into the actual sidewalk below, turning the whole city into a perceptual loop.

events.name: Spoon / Ratboys / BODEGA
events.why: Free, outdoors, tonight — Spoon headlining with Brooklyn post-punk anti-consumerists BODEGA opening; three bands with actual ideas about craft and social texture, not just a crowd-filler.

events.name: Mavis Staples / Brother Wallace
events.venue: SummerStage, Rumsey Playfield, Central Park (presented with The Apollo Theater)
events.why: At 86, Mavis Staples is a living feedback loop between gospel, civil rights, and American myth — she's collaborated with everyone from Bob Dylan to Prince — and this is a free outdoor performance with the Apollo Theater's institutional weight behind it; a rare convergence of deep time and living presence.

events.name: Complexions Contemporary Ballet / Paul Taylor Dance Company / LaTasha Barnes' The Jazz Continuum
events.venue: SummerStage, Rumsey Playfield, Central Park (presented with the Joyce Theater)
events.why: Three companies in one free evening: Complexions pushes ballet into hip-hop and contemporary hybrid territory, Paul Taylor's postmodern American canon, and LaTasha Barnes reframing jazz vernacular as choreographic philosophy — a single night that maps the entire argument about what bodies and social forms can do together.
